title: Cat Open Mouth Breathing: Is it an Emergency? | Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is breathing with an open mouth while at rest, it is a high-priority medical emergency. Learn why and what to do immediately.
content_html:
What is Open Mouth Breathing in Cats?

Open-mouth breathing, often looking like panting, occurs when a cat breathes through their mouth instead of their nose. Unlike dogs, who pant to regulate temperature, cats rarely breathe this way. When a cat is breathing with an open mouth while resting, it is a sign that they are struggling significantly to move air into their lungs or that their blood is not receiving enough oxygen.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, this is a life-threatening emergency. Because cats are evolutionarily designed to hide signs of weakness, open-mouth breathing at rest often indicates that the cat has reached a critical point of respiratory or cardiovascular distress. This could be caused by heart failure, asthma, pneumonia, or fluid in the chest. You should transport your cat to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

If you can do so without delaying your departure, a brief 5 to 10-second video of your cat's breathing can be vital for the veterinary team. It allows the triage staff to see the exact pattern of the breathing—such as abdominal effort or nostril flaring—which may change once the cat is stressed by the car ride or the clinic environment. This visual evidence helps the vet determine the severity of the crisis the moment you walk through the door.

faqs.question: Can heat cause my cat to breathe with an open mouth?
faqs.answer: While extreme heat can cause panting, it is much less common in cats than dogs. If your cat is in a cool environment and still breathing with an open mouth, it is a medical emergency.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the car cool and quiet. Minimize handling to reduce stress, as extra exertion can worsen their oxygen deficiency. Do not attempt to give water or food.

faqs.question: Are there other signs of breathing trouble to look for?
faqs.answer: Yes, look for blue, purple, or very pale gums, as well as a 'hunched' posture where the cat's elbows are pointed outward to help expand their chest.
